---
title: "Replication — Eval"
---

## Goal

Show the test-set performance of the four trained models on the held-out
final 12 months. Numbers below are loaded from the metrics CSV produced
by `evaluator.py` — they're the same numbers reported in the
[Results](../paper/04-results.qmd) chapter.

## Performance table

```{python}
#| label: tbl-perf
#| tbl-cap: "Test-set performance across the four models on the held-out final 12 months."
#| echo: false

import pandas as pd
metrics = pd.read_csv("../tables/performance_metrics.csv")
metrics.style.format({
    "RMSE": "{:.2f}",
    "MAE": "{:.2f}",
    "R2": "{:.4f}",
    "MAPE (%)": "{:.2f}",
})
```

## Feature–target correlations

```{python}
#| label: tbl-corr
#| tbl-cap: "Pearson correlation of each engineered feature with log(1 + cases)."
#| echo: false

import pandas as pd
corr = pd.read_csv("../tables/lag_correlation.csv")
corr.style.format({"Pearson_r_with_log_cases": "{:.3f}"})
```

## Interpretation

- The **Weighted Ensemble** is the headline model (R² = 0.8675, lowest
  RMSE). Its advantage over individual models comes from variance
  reduction across structurally distinct algorithms.
- **XGBoost** outperforms Random Forest on RMSE and MAE but has a
  higher MAPE — meaning it produces tighter absolute errors but is
  more proportionally biased on the smaller case-count districts.
- **MLP** (fallback for unavailable TF/LSTM) is competitive, achieving
  the lowest single-model RMSE but at higher absolute-error variance
  than the tree-based methods.

## Where the trained pickles live

Loaded as needed by the production pipeline:

```text
/Users/labaik/Desktop/Invoices/k/alldocumentsrelatedtoresearch/
├── rf_model.pkl
├── xgb_model.pkl
├── mlp_model.pkl
├── scaler_X.pkl
└── scaler_y.pkl
```

To regenerate from scratch, see [Train](train.qmd).

::: {.callout-tip}
The figures supporting this evaluation — actual-vs-predicted scatter,
feature importance ranking, seasonal boxplot — are on the
[Figures](../figures.qmd) page.
:::
